prompt: Explain what "meaning" means. Not "the meaning of X" — the concept itself.
response:
“Meaning” is the invisible glue that turns a raw happening into something *about* something else.  
It is the direction that signs, thoughts, words, gestures, or states of affairs acquire when they point beyond themselves—when they are *of* or *for* or *toward* something else. Without that pointing, there is only noise, shape, or motion; with it, the same physical stuff becomes a message, a warning, a promise, a joke, a law, a prayer.

Meaning is therefore not an extra ingredient mixed into the world; it is the pattern of *relations* by which parts of the world become intelligible *to* parts of the world. A cloud is just condensed water until, for a human eye, it is “about to rain.” The neural firing is just electrochemistry until, for the organism that owns it, it is “about food.” The ink mark is just graphite until, for a reader, it is “about justice.” In every case, the same matter is re-constellated by a perspective that treats it as *carrying import for something that matters to that perspective*.  

Because the import is always relative to a perspective, meaning is ternary: it needs (1) a sign or event, (2) an interpreting system (a person, a cell, a culture, a computer, a genome), and (3) the consequences that the interpreter would undergo if it took the sign one way rather than another. Strip away any leg of that tripod and “meaning” collapses back into mere physics.

So the concept itself is the name we give to the *directedness* that emerges when a difference in one domain can make a difference in another domain *for* an agent that has something at stake. It is the basic form of *being about*, prior to any specific content we might go on to label “the meaning of X.”
